section.title: Joash Repairs the Temple
section.sectionRationale: Joash, raised under Jehoiada's godly instruction, oversees a major repair of the LORD's temple, instituting a trustworthy system for collecting and disbursing funds that requires no accounting because the workers act with integrity. Yet when Aram's Hazael threatens Jerusalem, Joash strips the temple of its treasures to buy him off, and the chapter closes with his assassination by his own servants.
section.thesis: A king who does right as long as his mentor lives can still falter without lasting personal conviction, using God's house as a bargaining chip under pressure and finally dying at the hands of his own household, showing that inherited faithfulness is no substitute for a heart truly anchored in the LORD.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Joash's reign and the funding of temple repairs, verses 1 to 16; Hazael's threat and Joash's tribute from the temple treasures, verses 17 to 19; and the conspiracy that kills Joash, verses 20 to 21.

verses.text: In the seventh year of Jehu, Joash became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem forty years. His mother's name was Zibiah; she was from Beersheba.
And Joash did what was right in the eyes of the LORD all the days he was instructed by Jehoiada the priest.
Nevertheless, the high places were not removed; the people continued sacrificing and burning incense there.

Then Joash said to the priests, "Collect all the money brought as sacred gifts into the house of the LORD, the census money, the money from vows, and the money brought voluntarily into the house of the LORD.
Let every priest receive it from his constituency, and let it be used to repair any {{bedeq|damage}} found in the temple."
By the twenty-third year of the reign of Joash, however, the priests had not yet repaired the damage to the temple.

So King Joash called Jehoiada and the other priests and said, "Why have you not repaired the damage to the temple? Now, therefore, take no more money from your constituency, but hand it over for the repair of the temple."
So the priests agreed that they would not receive money from the people and that they would not repair the temple themselves.

Then Jehoiada the priest took a chest, bored a hole in its lid, and set it beside the altar on the right side as one enters the house of the LORD. There the priests who guarded the threshold put all the {{kesef|money}} brought into the house of the LORD.
Whenever they saw that there was a large amount of money in the chest, the royal scribe and the high priest would go up, count the money brought into the house of the LORD, and tie it up in bags.
Then they would put the counted money into the hands of those who supervised the work on the house of the LORD, who in turn would pay those doing the work, the carpenters, builders,
masons, and stonecutters. They also purchased timber and dressed stone to repair the damage to the house of the LORD, and they paid the other expenses of the temple repairs.
However, the money brought into the house of the LORD was not used for making silver basins, wick trimmers, sprinkling bowls, trumpets, or any articles of gold or silver for the house of the LORD.
Instead, it was paid to those doing the work, and with it they repaired the house of the LORD.
No accounting was required from the men who received the money to pay the workmen, because they acted with {{emunah|integrity}}.
The money from the {{asham|guilt offerings}} and sin offerings was not brought into the house of the LORD; it belonged to the priests.

units.label: Hazael's threat and the temple's treasures

verses.text: At that time Hazael king of Aram marched up and fought against Gath and captured it. Then he decided to attack Jerusalem.

So King Joash of Judah took all the sacred objects dedicated by his fathers, Jehoshaphat, Jehoram, and Ahaziah, the kings of Judah, along with his own consecrated items and all the gold found in the treasuries of the house of the LORD and the royal palace, and he sent them to Hazael king of Aram. So Hazael withdrew from Jerusalem.
As for the rest of the acts of Joash, along with all his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
And the servants of Joash rose up and formed a conspiracy and killed him at Beth-millo, on the road down to Silla.
His servants Jozabad son of Shimeath and Jehozabad son of Shomer struck him down, and he died. And they buried him with his fathers in the City of David, and his son Amaziah reigned in his place.

bedeq.gloss: Damage, breach, or repair, used of structural decay needing restoration. Joash's insistence that sacred funds be used specifically to repair this bedeq shows his genuine concern for the physical honor of the LORD's house, even while the high places, a deeper form of damage, remained untouched throughout his reign.

kesef.gloss: Silver or money generally. The careful, transparent system Jehoiada establishes for collecting and disbursing kesef for temple repairs, requiring no independent accounting because of the workers' proven integrity, stands in sharp, tragic contrast to Joash's later use of temple silver as tribute to buy off Hazael.

emunah.gloss: Faithfulness, steadiness, or integrity. The text notes explicitly that no accounting was required of the temple supervisors because they acted with emunah, a striking commendation of ordinary faithfulness that the king himself, in relying on inherited instruction rather than his own settled conviction, ultimately failed to sustain.

asham.gloss: Guilt offering, a specific sacrifice for particular sins requiring restitution, Leviticus 5 to 7. That asham and sin offering money belonged to the priests rather than funding repairs shows careful, established distinctions in how different sacred funds were designated and used under the law.

basic.context: Joash, rescued as an infant in the previous chapter, reigns forty years and does right in the LORD's eyes as long as Jehoiada the priest instructs him, though the high places are never removed. He institutes a careful, trustworthy system for collecting temple funds and repairing the LORD's house, a system so reliable that the honest workers required no financial oversight at all. Yet when Hazael of Aram threatens Jerusalem after capturing Gath, Joash strips the temple and palace of their sacred and royal treasures to buy him off rather than trusting the LORD for deliverance. The chapter ends starkly with Joash's own servants assassinating him.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting a specific king's mixed reign; it is not a template for church fundraising or building repair, though its picture of integrity in handling sacred resources remains instructive. What endures is the sobering truth that faithfulness sustained by another's influence, rather than one's own settled conviction, often falters under pressure, and that using what belongs to God as a bargaining chip reflects a heart not fully anchored in trust.
basic.originalLanguages: Joash is genuinely concerned to repair the bedeq, damage, in the LORD's house, and the system Jehoiada sets up for the kesef, money, involved is handled with such emunah, faithfulness, that no audit was ever required. Yet by the chapter's end this same king empties the temple treasuries to appease Hazael, a jarring contrast that exposes how thin his own personal devotion, apart from Jehoiada's ongoing instruction, may have been.
basic.typology: The temple repaired through honest stewardship, only to be later stripped of its treasures under external threat, previews the greater and lasting temple, the very presence of God dwelling among His people in Christ, which no invading enemy or human failure can ever plunder or diminish.
basic.christ: The careful integrity shown by ordinary workers repairing the LORD's earthly house anticipates the far greater faithfulness of Christ, the true and perfect Temple, John 2:19-21, who, unlike Joash, never compromised what belonged to God under any pressure, and who cleansed rather than despoiled His Father's house, John 2:14-17.
basic.god: God delights in honest, faithful stewardship of what is dedicated to Him, commending the ordinary integrity of temple workers who needed no oversight. Yet He is not honored when even sacred and consecrated resources are used merely as leverage to escape difficulty rather than as an occasion to trust Him, and Joash's declining fortunes after this compromise reveal the seriousness of that failure.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Chronicles 24 gives a fuller account of this same reign, including Joash's later turn to idolatry and his murder of Jehoiada's own son Zechariah, deepening the tragedy only summarized briefly here. 1 Kings 15:18 records an earlier king similarly stripping the temple to buy foreign help, showing this compromise was a recurring temptation for Judah's kings. John 2:13-17 shows Christ's very different, zealous protection of His Father's house.
basic.oneStory: Joash's reign begins in genuine, mentored faithfulness and ends in compromise and assassination, illustrating the whole book's recurring pattern that inherited or externally sustained obedience cannot substitute for a heart truly devoted to the LORD, a pattern only broken by Christ, whose faithfulness to His Father's house never wavered under any pressure.

context.literary: The chapter moves from an extended, positive account of careful temple administration to a brief, jarring account of compromise and assassination, a literary structure that mirrors the trajectory of Joash's entire reign as summarized here and expanded in Chronicles.
context.historical: Temple funding through census money, vow offerings, and freewill gifts reflects established Israelite practice, Exodus 30:11-16, and the sealed chest system Jehoiada devises represents a genuinely innovative solution to a documented, decades-long failure by the priests to complete repairs. Hazael's campaign against Gath and threat to Jerusalem corresponds to broader, independently attested Aramean expansion under Hazael during this era.
context.canonical: This chapter's brief mention of assassination gains its full weight from 2 Chronicles 24, which reveals Joash later abandoned the LORD after Jehoiada's death and had Jehoiada's own son murdered, showing that the summary judgment here, doing right only during Jehoiada's instruction, was a deliberate and pointed qualification by the author of Kings.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Joash's faithfulness, genuine as it was during Jehoiada's lifetime, depended on external instruction rather than settled personal conviction, and that this pattern led first to compromising the temple's treasures under pressure and finally to his own violent death.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical king's administration and its eventual failure; it does not prescribe the exact financial system used for temple repairs as a required model, though its principle of transparent, trustworthy stewardship remains a wise pattern. It also does not endorse Joash's later decision to buy off Hazael with sacred treasures, which the narrative implicitly critiques by its placement immediately before his assassination.

debates.question: Was Joash's decision to pay off Hazael with temple treasures, verse 18, a wise, pragmatic act of statecraft or a failure of faith?

views.label: A pragmatic, defensible act of statecraft to spare Jerusalem from destruction
views.case: Facing a a genuinely formidable military threat after Hazael's capture of Gath, using available treasury resources to avert a siege and preserve the city and temple itself from destruction could be read as a prudent, if costly, act of practical wisdom rather than outright unfaithfulness.

views.label: A failure of faith that treats the LORD's house as a mere bargaining chip
views.case: The narrative's placement of this event immediately before recounting Joash's assassination, along with the broader biblical pattern of judging kings who strip the temple to buy foreign favor rather than trusting the LORD, 1 Kings 15:18-19, suggests the author intends readers to see this as a telling failure of trust rather than commendable pragmatism.

debates.bearing: Either way, the episode marks a clear turning point in the narrative, moving from a portrait of careful, faithful stewardship to one of costly compromise, immediately followed by Joash's tragic end.

study.originalLanguages: Joash directs real, sustained effort toward repairing the bedeq, damage, in the LORD's house, and the kesef, money, gathered for that purpose is handled with such emunah, faithfulness, that the workers require no oversight at all, a striking biblical commendation of ordinary integrity. Yet this same king later empties the treasuries, including funds never intended for such use, since asham, guilt offering, money was reserved separately for the priests, showing how far his final decisions departed from the careful, principled stewardship that marked his earlier reign.

typology.type: A temple carefully repaired through honest stewardship yet later stripped under external threat
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true and greater Temple, whose glory and faithfulness no threat or human failure can ever diminish or plunder, John 2:19-21.

typology.type: Faithfulness sustained by another's ongoing instruction rather than settled personal conviction
typology.fulfillment: The New Covenant promise of God's law written directly on the heart, producing faithfulness that does not depend on an external mentor's presence, Jeremiah 31:33, Hebrews 8:10.

study.christ: The temple, repaired through the honest stewardship of ordinary workers who required no oversight, points ahead to Christ, the true and perfect Temple, John 2:19-21, whose own zeal for His Father's house led Him to cleanse rather than strip it, John 2:14-17. Where Joash's faithfulness proved dependent on Jehoiada's ongoing instruction and ultimately faltered under pressure, Christ's obedience to the Father never wavered under any threat, and where Joash used sacred treasures to buy temporary safety, Christ gave His own life, the costliest treasure of all, to secure permanent salvation for His people.
study.god: God delights in honest, careful stewardship of what is dedicated to Him, commending workers whose integrity required no financial oversight at all. Yet He is not honored when even consecrated resources become merely a means of escaping difficulty rather than an occasion for trusting Him fully, and the narrative's placement of Joash's temple-stripping compromise immediately before his assassination points to the serious, ultimately corrosive consequences of faith that depends on another's influence rather than one's own settled conviction.

crossReferences.note: Gives the fuller account of Joash's reign, including his later turn to idolatry after Jehoiada's death and the murder of Jehoiada's own son Zechariah.
An earlier king of Judah similarly strips the temple treasury to buy foreign military help, showing this was a recurring and questionable pattern among Judah's kings.
Christ's zealous cleansing, rather than stripping, of His Father's house shows the very different faithfulness of the true and greater Temple.
The New Covenant promise of God's law written on the heart, producing a faithfulness not dependent on another's ongoing external instruction.

study.oneStory: Joash's reign begins with genuine, carefully administered faithfulness to repair the LORD's house, sustained by his mentor Jehoiada's instruction, yet ends in compromise, as sacred treasures are used to buy off a foreign threat, and finally in his own assassination, illustrating the whole book's recurring pattern that inherited obedience is no substitute for a heart truly anchored in the LORD, a pattern broken only by Christ, the true Temple whose faithfulness never wavered and whose sacrifice, not compromise, secures lasting peace for His people.

quiz.q: For how long did Joash do what was right in the eyes of the LORD, according to the text?

options.text: All the days he was instructed by Jehoiada the priest, a qualification that implies his faithfulness was tied to his mentor's ongoing influence.
options.note: Verse 2 gives this exact, pointed qualification of his righteous reign.

options.text: His entire forty-year reign without any qualification.
options.note: The text specifically ties his righteousness to the period of Jehoiada's instruction, not his whole reign unconditionally.

options.text: Only during the first year of his reign as an infant king.
options.note: The qualification concerns Jehoiada's instruction, which continued well beyond his infancy, not merely a single first year.

options.text: The text states he never did what was right at all.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly commends his righteousness during the period of Jehoiada's instruction.

quiz.q: How did Jehoiada finally solve the problem of the priests failing to repair the temple?

options.text: He set up a chest with a hole in its lid beside the altar to collect money directly, which was then counted and paid straight to the workers without passing through the priests' hands for repairs.
options.note: Verses 9 to 11 describe this exact solution after the priests' initial failure.

options.text: He simply increased the tax burden on the general population.
options.note: The solution was a more direct, transparent collection and disbursement system, not an increased tax burden.

options.text: He removed all the priests from their positions entirely.
options.note: The priests continued in their roles; the change was in how money was collected and used for repairs, not their removal.

options.text: The temple repairs were never actually completed.
options.note: The text describes carpenters, builders, masons, and stonecutters being paid and the repairs proceeding under the new system.

quiz.q: Why did the men handling the repair funds not need to give an accounting of the money?

options.text: Because they acted with integrity, a trust the text explicitly commends.
options.note: Verse 15 states this exact reason for the lack of required accounting.

options.text: Because no one in Jerusalem cared how the money was spent.
options.note: The text attributes the lack of accounting specifically to the workers' proven integrity, not general indifference.

options.text: Because the amounts involved were too small to matter.
options.note: Substantial repairs, timber, dressed stone, and multiple trades of workers, were funded, not a small or trivial sum.

options.text: Because the king forbade any kind of financial record keeping.
options.note: The text attributes this specifically to trustworthiness, not a royal prohibition on records.

quiz.q: How did Joash respond when Hazael king of Aram threatened Jerusalem after capturing Gath?

options.text: He stripped the temple and palace treasuries of sacred and royal treasures and sent them to Hazael, who then withdrew from Jerusalem.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 record this exact response and its immediate result.

options.text: He led Judah's army out to a decisive military victory over Hazael.
options.note: The text describes a payment of treasures to secure withdrawal, not a military victory.

options.text: He refused to give Hazael anything and Jerusalem was destroyed.
options.note: Joash did comply with tribute, and the text states Hazael withdrew rather than destroying the city.

options.text: Hazael never actually threatened Jerusalem at all.
options.note: The text explicitly states Hazael decided to attack Jerusalem before Joash's tribute.

options.text: His own servants formed a conspiracy and assassinated him at Beth-millo.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 record this assassination by name, identifying his own servants as the conspirators.

options.text: He died peacefully of old age after a long and stable reign.
options.note: The text explicitly describes a violent conspiracy and assassination, not a peaceful natural death.

options.text: He was defeated and killed in battle against Aram.
options.note: His death came at the hands of his own servants in a domestic conspiracy, not in battle against a foreign enemy.

options.text: The text does not describe how his reign ended.
options.note: Verses 20 to 21 give a specific and detailed account of his assassination.

questions: Joash did right as long as Jehoiada instructed him, suggesting his obedience may have depended more on his mentor's influence than his own settled conviction. Whose faith or example are you currently leaning on that you need to make more truly your own?

The temple workers were trusted with money and required no oversight because of their proven integrity. What would it look like for your own reputation for trustworthiness to grow to that same degree in an area of your life right now?

Joash used sacred treasures to buy temporary safety from a real threat rather than seeking the LORD directly. Where might you be tempted to use something valuable or sacred as a shortcut around trusting God in a difficult situation?

The chapter moves quickly from careful faithfulness to compromise to assassination, a sobering trajectory. What warning signs might indicate that your own faithfulness is beginning to drift, before it reaches a serious crisis point?

This chapter shows genuine good works, like temple repair, are not the same as a fully surrendered heart. Where in your life might you be doing good, visible things while an underlying area of trust or obedience remains unaddressed?

translationNotes.note: The three categories of funds named, census money, vow money, and voluntary gifts, correspond to specific offerings prescribed in the Law, showing a genuine attempt to properly collect designated sacred funds.
The chest with a hole bored in its lid represents an early and notable example of a secure, transparent public collection system, functioning much like a modern donation box.

Beth-millo, meaning house of the fill or rampart, was likely a fortified section of the royal complex, and the specific naming of the conspirators, Jozabad and Jehozabad, marks this as a documented, remembered act of regicide rather than an anonymous uprising.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 12:1-21. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
